title: Die zwölf Brüder
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 9. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The ninth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 9): a king means to kill his twelve sons should a daughter be born, but the boys flee to the forest, and the sister who is born after them grows up, finds them, and by an innocent act turns them into ravens. To redeem them she must stay seven years mute and never laugh — a vow that, while she is a king’s bride, nearly sends her to the stake on a wicked mother-in-law’s slander, until the last instant of the seventh year sets her brothers free. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng/fieng/giengen, ward, that (for tat), hub, Reichthum, Thurm, rothe, getödtet, Oel and Todtenkißchen.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein König und eine Königin, die lebten in Frieden mit einander und hatten zwölf Kinder, das waren aber lauter Buben.
sentences.l: There was once a king and a queen, who lived in peace with one another and had twelve children, but these were all boys.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there were a king and a queen who lived in peace together and had twelve children, but they were all sons.
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ “lauter” here means ‘nothing but, all’ — the children were all boys. “Buben” = boys, lads.

sentences.t: Nun sprach der König zu seiner Frau „wenn das dreizehnte Kind, was du zur Welt bringst, ein Mädchen ist, so sollen die zwölf Buben sterben, damit sein Reichthum groß wird und das Königreich ihm allein zufällt.“
sentences.l: Now the king spoke to his wife, “if the thirteenth child that you bring into the world is a girl, then the twelve boys shall die, so that her wealth grows great and the kingdom falls to her alone.”
sentences.i: Now the king said to his wife, “If the thirteenth child you bring into the world is a girl, then the twelve boys shall die, so that her wealth may be great and the kingdom fall to her alone.”
sentences.n: “zur Welt bringen” = to bring into the world, give birth to. “Reichthum” carries the old -th- (modern Reichtum). “zufällt” is the separable verb zufallen (‘to fall to, pass to’) taking the dative ihm — the kingdom passes to her (grammatically ‘him,’ das Kind being neuter... here ihm refers to the daughter as heir).

sentences.t: Er ließ auch zwölf Särge machen, die waren schon mit Hobelspänen gefüllt, und in jedem lag das Todtenkißchen, und ließ sie in eine verschlossene Stube bringen, dann gab er der Königin den Schlüssel und gebot ihr niemand etwas davon zu sagen.
sentences.l: He also had twelve coffins made, which were already filled with wood-shavings, and in each lay the little death-pillow, and had them brought into a locked chamber; then he gave the queen the key and bade her tell no one anything of it.
sentences.i: He also had twelve coffins made, already filled with wood-shavings, and in each lay a little death-pillow; and he had them carried into a locked room, then gave the queen the key and charged her to tell no one about it.
sentences.n: “ließ ... machen / bringen” — lassen + infinitive means ‘to have (something) done.’ “Todtenkißchen” (1857 Todten- for Toten-) is a little pillow laid in a coffin. “gebot” is the preterite of gebieten, ‘to command, bid.’

sentences.t: Die Mutter aber saß nun den ganzen Tag und trauerte, so daß der kleinste Sohn, der immer bei ihr war, und den sie nach der Bibel Benjamin nannte, zu ihr sprach „liebe Mutter, warum bist du so traurig?“
sentences.l: But the mother now sat the whole day and mourned, so that the smallest son, who was always with her, and whom she named Benjamin after the Bible, spoke to her, “dear mother, why are you so sad?”
sentences.i: But the mother now sat grieving all day long, so that the youngest son, who was always with her, and whom she had named Benjamin after the Bible, said to her, “Dear mother, why are you so sad?”
sentences.n: “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass. “nach der Bibel” — Benjamin is the youngest son of Jacob in Genesis, so the name marks the youngest child. “trauerte” = mourned, grieved.

sentences.t: „Liebstes Kind,“ antwortete sie, „ich darf dirs nicht sagen.“
sentences.l: “Dearest child,” she answered, “I may not tell it to you.”
sentences.i: “Dearest child,” she answered, “I dare not tell you.”
sentences.n: “darf” is dürfen, ‘to be allowed to, may.’ “dirs” = dir es (‘it to you’), a contraction the edition writes solid.

sentences.t: Er ließ ihr aber keine Ruhe, bis sie gieng und die Stube aufschloß, und ihm die zwölf mit Hobelspänen schon gefüllten Todtenladen zeigte.
sentences.l: But he let her no peace, until she went and unlocked the chamber, and showed him the twelve coffins already filled with wood-shavings.
sentences.i: But he gave her no peace, until she went and unlocked the room and showed him the twelve coffins already filled with wood-shavings.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (this -ie- spelling recurs: gieng, fieng, giengen). “aufschloß” is aufschließen (‘to unlock’), separable. “Todtenladen” (Todtenlade) is another word for coffins, ‘death-chests.’

sentences.t: Darauf sprach sie „mein liebster Benjamin, diese Särge hat dein Vater für dich und deine elf Brüder machen lassen, denn wenn ich ein Mädchen zur Welt bringe, so sollt ihr allesammt getödtet und darin begraben werden.“
sentences.l: Thereupon she spoke, “my dearest Benjamin, these coffins your father had made for you and your eleven brothers, for if I bring a girl into the world, then you shall all be killed and buried in them.”
sentences.i: Then she said, “My dearest Benjamin, your father had these coffins made for you and your eleven brothers, for if I bring a girl into the world, you shall all be killed and buried in them.”
sentences.n: “hat ... machen lassen” = had (them) made (lassen + infinitive). “allesammt” = all together, every one. “getödtet” carries the old -öd-/-th- spelling (modern getötet).

sentences.t: Und als sie weinte während sie das sprach, so tröstete sie der Sohn und sagte „weine nicht, liebe Mutter, wir wollen uns schon helfen und wollen fortgehen.“
sentences.l: And as she wept while she spoke that, the son comforted her and said, “weep not, dear mother, we will surely help ourselves and will go away.”
sentences.i: And as she wept while she said this, the son comforted her and said, “Do not weep, dear mother, we will manage well enough, and will go away.”
sentences.n: “wir wollen uns schon helfen” — the particle schon here means ‘surely, well enough,’ reassuring. “fortgehen” = to go away, depart.

sentences.t: Sie aber sprach „geh mit deinen elf Brüdern hinaus in den Wald, und einer setze sich immer auf den höchsten Baum, der zu finden ist, und halte Wacht und schaue nach dem Thurm hier im Schloß.
sentences.l: But she spoke, “go out with your eleven brothers into the wood, and let one always seat himself on the highest tree that is to be found, and keep watch and look toward the tower here in the castle.
sentences.i: But she said, “Go out with your eleven brothers into the forest, and let one of you always climb the tallest tree to be found, and keep watch, and look toward the tower here in the castle.
sentences.n: “setze sich ... halte ... schaue” are third-person imperatives (‘let one seat himself ... keep ... look’). “Wacht halten” = to keep watch. “Thurm” carries the old -th- (modern Turm).

sentences.t: Gebär ich ein Söhnlein, so will ich eine weiße Fahne aufstecken, und dann dürft ihr wiederkommen; gebär ich ein Töchterlein, so will ich eine rothe Fahne aufstecken, und dann flieht fort, so schnell ihr könnt, und der liebe Gott behüte euch.
sentences.l: Should I bear a little son, then I will put up a white flag, and then you may come back; should I bear a little daughter, then I will put up a red flag, and then flee away, as fast as you can, and may the dear God protect you.
sentences.i: If I bear a little son, I will hoist a white flag, and then you may come back; if I bear a little daughter, I will hoist a red flag, and then flee away as fast as you can, and may the dear Lord keep you.
sentences.n: “Gebär ich ...” — verb-first is a conditional, ‘if / should I bear ...’ (gebären, to give birth). “Söhnlein” and “Töchterlein” are diminutives (little son / little daughter). “aufstecken” (to put up, hoist) is separable. “behüte” is a present subjunctive of wish: ‘may God guard you.’

sentences.t: Alle Nacht will ich aufstehen und für euch beten, im Winter, daß ihr an einem Feuer euch wärmen könnt, im Sommer, daß ihr nicht in der Hitze schmachtet.“
sentences.l: Every night I will get up and pray for you, in winter, that you may warm yourselves at a fire, in summer, that you do not languish in the heat.”
sentences.i: Every night I will rise and pray for you — in winter, that you may warm yourselves at a fire; in summer, that you may not languish in the heat.”
sentences.n: “Alle Nacht” = every night. “aufstehen” (to get up) is separable. “schmachten” = to languish, pine, suffer (here from heat).

sentences.t: Nachdem sie also ihre Söhne gesegnet hatte, giengen sie hinaus in den Wald.
sentences.l: After she had thus blessed her sons, they went out into the wood.
sentences.i: So after she had blessed her sons, they went out into the forest.
sentences.n: “Nachdem ... gesegnet hatte” = after she had blessed (pluperfect). “giengen” is the 1857 spelling of gingen (‘went’).

sentences.t: Einer hielt um den andern Wacht, saß auf der höchsten Eiche und schauete nach dem Thurm.
sentences.l: One kept watch for the other in turn, sat on the highest oak and looked toward the tower.
sentences.i: They kept watch by turns, each sitting in the tallest oak and looking toward the tower.
sentences.n: “einer um den andern” = one after another, in turns. “schauete” is an older preterite of schauen (modern schaute), ‘gazed, looked.’ “Eiche” = oak.

sentences.t: Als elf Tage herum waren und die Reihe an Benjamin kam, da sah er wie eine Fahne aufgesteckt wurde: es war aber nicht die weiße sondern die rothe Blutfahne, die verkündigte daß sie alle sterben sollten.
sentences.l: When eleven days were round and the turn came to Benjamin, then he saw how a flag was put up: but it was not the white one, rather the red blood-flag, which proclaimed that they should all die.
sentences.i: When eleven days had gone round and it was Benjamin’s turn, he saw a flag being hoisted — but it was not the white one, but the red blood-flag, which proclaimed that they must all die.
sentences.n: “die Reihe kommt an jemand” = it is someone’s turn. “nicht ... sondern” = ‘not ... but rather.’ “Blutfahne” = blood-flag. “verkündigte” = proclaimed, announced.

sentences.t: Wie die Brüder das hörten, wurden sie zornig und sprachen „sollten wir um eines Mädchens willen den Tod leiden! wir schwören daß wir uns rächen wollen: wo wir ein Mädchen finden, soll sein rothes Blut fließen.“
sentences.l: When the brothers heard that, they grew angry and spoke, “should we suffer death for a girl’s sake! we swear that we will avenge ourselves: wherever we find a girl, her red blood shall flow.”
sentences.i: When the brothers heard this, they grew furious and said, “Are we to suffer death for the sake of a girl! We swear that we will take our revenge: wherever we find a girl, her red blood shall flow.”
sentences.n: “um ... willen” = for the sake of (framing a genitive: um eines Mädchens willen). “schwören” = to swear, vow. “sein rothes Blut” — sein is neuter agreeing with das Mädchen, so ‘its/her red blood.’ “rothes” has the old -th- (modern rotes).

sentences.t: Darauf giengen sie tiefer in den Wald hinein, und mitten drein, wo er am dunkelsten war, fanden sie ein kleines verwünschtes Häuschen, das leer stand.
sentences.l: Thereupon they went deeper into the wood, and right in the midst, where it was darkest, they found a little enchanted cottage that stood empty.
sentences.i: Then they went deeper into the forest, and in the very midst of it, where it was darkest, they found a little enchanted cottage standing empty.
sentences.n: “mitten drein” = right into the middle. “am dunkelsten” = at the darkest (superlative). “verwünscht” = enchanted, bewitched — this cottage recurs through the tale. “Häuschen” = little house, cottage.

sentences.t: Da sprachen sie „hier wollen wir wohnen, und du, Benjamin, du bist der jüngste und schwächste, du sollst daheim bleiben und haushalten, wir andern wollen ausgehen und Essen holen.“
sentences.l: Then they spoke, “here we will dwell, and you, Benjamin, you are the youngest and weakest, you shall stay at home and keep house, we others will go out and fetch food.”
sentences.i: Then they said, “Here we will live, and you, Benjamin — you are the youngest and weakest — you shall stay at home and keep house, and we others will go out and fetch food.”
sentences.n: “der jüngste und schwächste” = the youngest and weakest (superlatives). “haushalten” = to keep house, manage the household (separable). “daheim” = at home.

sentences.t: Nun zogen sie in den Wald und schossen Hasen, wilde Rehe, Vögel und Täuberchen und was zu essen stand: das brachten sie dem Benjamin, der mußte es ihnen zurecht machen, damit sie ihren Hunger stillen konnten.
sentences.l: Now they went off into the wood and shot hares, wild roe-deer, birds and little doves and whatever was fit to eat: this they brought to Benjamin, who had to prepare it for them, so that they could still their hunger.
sentences.i: Now they went off into the forest and shot hares, wild roe, birds and young doves and whatever was fit to eat; this they brought to Benjamin, who had to make it ready for them, so that they could satisfy their hunger.
sentences.n: “was zu essen stand” = whatever was to be eaten, was fit for food. “zurecht machen” = to make ready, prepare. “den Hunger stillen” = to still / satisfy hunger. “Täuberchen” = little doves (diminutive of Taube).

sentences.t: In dem Häuschen lebten sie zehn Jahre zusammen, und die Zeit ward ihnen nicht lang.
sentences.l: In the cottage they lived ten years together, and the time did not grow long to them.
sentences.i: In the cottage they lived together ten years, and the time did not seem long to them.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became’); modern German uses wurde — you meet ward throughout Grimm. “die Zeit ward ihnen nicht lang” = the time did not drag for them (dative ihnen, ‘to them’).

sentences.t: Das Töchterchen, das ihre Mutter, die Königin, geboren hatte, war nun herangewachsen, war gut von Herzen und schön von Angesicht und hatte einen goldenen Stern auf der Stirne.
sentences.l: The little daughter, whom her mother, the queen, had borne, had now grown up, was good of heart and fair of face and had a golden star on her forehead.
sentences.i: The little daughter whom her mother, the queen, had borne had now grown up; she was good of heart and fair of face, and had a golden star on her brow.
sentences.n: “Töchterchen” = little daughter (diminutive). “herangewachsen” is the past participle of heranwachsen (‘to grow up’), separable. “gut von Herzen ... schön von Angesicht” = good of heart, fair of face. “Stern auf der Stirne” = star on the forehead (Stirne, old form of Stirn).

sentences.t: Einmal, als große Wäsche war, sah es darunter zwölf Mannshemden und fragte seine Mutter „wem gehören diese zwölf Hemden, für den Vater sind sie doch viel zu klein?“
sentences.l: Once, when there was a big wash, it saw among it twelve men’s shirts and asked its mother, “to whom do these twelve shirts belong? for father they are surely much too small.”
sentences.i: Once, on a big wash-day, she saw among the laundry twelve men’s shirts and asked her mother, “Whose are these twelve shirts? They are surely far too small for father.”
sentences.n: “es” refers to the daughter as das Töchterchen/Mädchen (neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ — read as ‘she.’ “große Wäsche” = the big wash (laundry day). “wem gehören” — gehören takes the dative: ‘to whom do they belong.’ “Mannshemden” = men’s shirts.

sentences.t: Da antwortete sie mit schwerem Herzen „liebes Kind, die gehören deinen zwölf Brüdern.“
sentences.l: Then she answered with a heavy heart, “dear child, they belong to your twelve brothers.”
sentences.n: “mit schwerem Herzen” = with a heavy heart. “gehören deinen ... Brüdern” — gehören + dative, ‘belong to your brothers.’

sentences.t: Sprach das Mädchen „wo sind meine zwölf Brüder, ich habe noch niemals von ihnen gehört.“
sentences.l: Spoke the girl, “where are my twelve brothers? I have never yet heard of them.”
sentences.i: The girl said, “Where are my twelve brothers? I have never even heard of them.”
sentences.n: “Sprach das Mädchen” — verb-first narration (‘said the girl’), common in Grimm dialogue. “niemals” = never; “noch niemals” = never yet.

sentences.t: Sie antwortete „das weiß Gott, wo sie sind: sie irren in der Welt herum.“
sentences.l: She answered, “God knows where they are: they wander about in the world.”
sentences.i: She answered, “God knows where they are — they are wandering somewhere in the world.”
sentences.n: “das weiß Gott” = God knows (that). “irren ... herum” = umherirren, to wander about, roam (separable, split here as irren ... herum).

sentences.t: Da nahm sie das Mädchen und schloß ihm das Zimmer auf, und zeigte ihm die zwölf Särge mit den Hobelspänen und den Todtenkißchen.
sentences.l: Then she took the girl and unlocked the room for her, and showed her the twelve coffins with the wood-shavings and the little death-pillows.
sentences.i: Then she took the girl and unlocked the room for her, and showed her the twelve coffins with their wood-shavings and little death-pillows.
sentences.n: “schloß ... auf” is aufschließen (‘to unlock’), separable, here past. “ihm” is dative neuter referring to das Mädchen — ‘for her.’ “Zimmer” = room, chamber.

sentences.t: „Diese Särge,“ sprach sie, „waren für deine Brüder bestimmt, aber sie sind heimlich fortgegangen, eh du geboren warst,“ und erzählte ihm wie sich alles zugetragen hatte.
sentences.l: “These coffins,” she spoke, “were destined for your brothers, but they went away secretly, ere you were born,” and told her how everything had come about.
sentences.i: “These coffins,” she said, “were meant for your brothers, but they secretly went away before you were born,” and she told her how it had all come to pass.
sentences.n: “für ... bestimmt” = destined / intended for. “eh” is a shortened, older form of ehe (‘before’). “sich zutragen” = to come about, happen — “wie sich alles zugetragen hatte,” how it all had come to pass.

sentences.t: Da sagte das Mädchen „liebe Mutter, weine nicht, ich will gehen und meine Brüder suchen.“
sentences.l: Then the girl said, “dear mother, weep not, I will go and seek my brothers.”
sentences.i: Then the girl said, “Dear mother, do not weep — I will go and search for my brothers.”

sentences.t: Nun nahm es die zwölf Hemden und gieng fort und geradezu in den großen Wald hinein.
sentences.l: Now it took the twelve shirts and went away and straight into the great wood.
sentences.i: Now she took the twelve shirts and went off, straight into the great forest.
sentences.n: “es” again = das Mädchen (neuter), ‘she.’ “gieng fort” (fortgehen) = went away. “geradezu ... hinein” = straight on into.

sentences.t: Es gieng den ganzen Tag und am Abend kam es zu dem verwünschten Häuschen.
sentences.l: It went the whole day and in the evening it came to the enchanted cottage.
sentences.i: She walked the whole day, and in the evening she came to the enchanted cottage.

sentences.t: Da trat es hinein und fand einen jungen Knaben, der fragte „wo kommst du her und wo willst du hin?“ und erstaunte daß sie so schön war, königliche Kleider trug und einen Stern auf der Stirne hatte.
sentences.l: Then it stepped inside and found a young boy, who asked, “where do you come from and where do you want to go?” and was amazed that she was so beautiful, wore royal clothes and had a star on her forehead.
sentences.i: Then she stepped inside and found a young boy, who asked, “Where do you come from, and where are you going?” and marvelled that she was so beautiful, wore royal clothes, and had a star on her brow.
sentences.n: “wo ... her ... wo ... hin” — German splits ‘whence’ and ‘whither’: woher (where from), wohin (where to), here separated as wo ... her / wo ... hin. Note the switch from neuter “es” to feminine “sie” once the narrator thinks of her as the girl herself.

sentences.t: Da antwortete sie „ich bin eine Königstochter und suche meine zwölf Brüder und will gehen so weit der Himmel blau ist, bis ich sie finde.“
sentences.l: Then she answered, “I am a king’s daughter and seek my twelve brothers and will go as far as the sky is blue, until I find them.”
sentences.i: Then she answered, “I am a king’s daughter, and I am seeking my twelve brothers, and I will go as far as the sky is blue, until I find them.”
sentences.n: “so weit der Himmel blau ist” = as far as the sky is blue — a folk idiom for ‘to the ends of the earth.’

sentences.t: Sie zeigte ihm auch die zwölf Hemden, die ihnen gehörten.
sentences.l: She also showed him the twelve shirts that belonged to them.
sentences.n: “die ihnen gehörten” — gehören + dative again, ‘that belonged to them.’

sentences.t: Da sah Benjamin daß es seine Schwester war und sprach „ich bin Benjamin, dein jüngster Bruder.“
sentences.l: Then Benjamin saw that it was his sister and spoke, “I am Benjamin, your youngest brother.”
sentences.i: Then Benjamin saw that she was his sister and said, “I am Benjamin, your youngest brother.”

sentences.t: Und sie fieng an zu weinen vor Freude, und Benjamin auch, und sie küßten und herzten einander vor großer Liebe.
sentences.l: And she began to weep for joy, and Benjamin too, and they kissed and embraced one another out of great love.
sentences.i: And she began to weep for joy, and Benjamin too, and they kissed and hugged one another out of great love.
sentences.n: “fieng an” is the 1857 spelling of fing an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable. “vor Freude / vor großer Liebe” — vor + dative for cause, ‘for joy, out of love.’ “herzten” (herzen) = to hug, embrace.

sentences.t: Hernach sprach er „liebe Schwester, es ist noch ein Vorbehalt da, wir hatten verabredet, daß ein jedes Mädchen, das uns begegnete, sterben sollte, weil wir um ein Mädchen unser Königreich verlassen mußten.“
sentences.l: Afterward he spoke, “dear sister, there is still a reservation, we had agreed that every girl who met us should die, because for a girl’s sake we had to leave our kingdom.”
sentences.i: Afterward he said, “Dear sister, there is still one condition: we had agreed that any girl who met us should die, because it was for a girl’s sake that we had to leave our kingdom.”
sentences.n: “Hernach” = afterward, thereafter. “Vorbehalt” = reservation, proviso, condition. “verabredet” (verabreden) = agreed, arranged. “um ein Mädchen” = for/on account of a girl.

sentences.t: Da sagte sie „ich will gerne sterben, wenn ich damit meine zwölf Brüder erlösen kann.“
sentences.l: Then she said, “I will gladly die, if with it I can redeem my twelve brothers.”
sentences.i: Then she said, “I will gladly die, if by it I can deliver my twelve brothers.”
sentences.n: “erlösen” = to redeem, deliver, free from a spell — a key word of this tale (she will later redeem them from raven form).

sentences.t: „Nein,“ antwortete er, „du sollst nicht sterben, setze dich unter diese Bütte bis die elf Brüder kommen, dann will ich schon einig mit ihnen werden.“
sentences.l: “No,” he answered, “you shall not die, seat yourself under this tub until the eleven brothers come, then I will surely come to agreement with them.”
sentences.i: “No,” he answered, “you shall not die; sit down under this tub until the eleven brothers come, and then I will surely come to terms with them.”
sentences.n: “Bütte” = a large tub or vat. “einig werden mit” = to come to agreement / terms with. The particle schon again reassures: ‘surely, well enough.’

sentences.t: Also that sie; und wie es Nacht ward, kamen die andern von der Jagd, und die Mahlzeit war bereit.
sentences.l: So did she; and when it became night, the others came from the hunt, and the meal was ready.
sentences.i: So she did; and when night fell, the others came home from the hunt, and the meal was ready.
sentences.n: “Also that sie” = ‘so did she’ — that is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = modern tun). “ward” again, the old preterite of werden.

sentences.t: Und als sie am Tische saßen und aßen, fragten sie „was gibts neues?“
sentences.l: And as they sat at the table and ate, they asked, “what’s new?”
sentences.i: And as they sat at the table eating, they asked, “What’s the news?”
sentences.n: “am Tische” has the old dative -e (modern am Tisch). “was gibts neues” = ‘what is there new?’ — gibts is es gibt + ’s.

sentences.t: Sprach Benjamin „wißt ihr nichts?“
sentences.l: Spoke Benjamin, “do you know nothing?”
sentences.i: Benjamin said, “Don’t you know anything?”
sentences.n: “wißt” is the 1857 spelling of wisst (wissen, ‘to know’), 2nd person plural.

sentences.t: „Nein“ antworteten sie.
sentences.l: “No,” they answered.

sentences.t: Sprach er weiter „ihr seid im Walde gewesen, und ich bin daheim geblieben, und weiß doch mehr als ihr.“
sentences.l: He spoke further, “you have been in the wood, and I have stayed at home, and yet know more than you.”
sentences.i: He went on, “You have been out in the forest, and I have stayed at home, and yet I know more than you.”
sentences.n: “im Walde” has the old dative -e. “doch” here = ‘yet, all the same.’

sentences.t: „So erzähle uns“ riefen sie.
sentences.l: “Then tell us,” they cried.
sentences.n: “erzähle” is the imperative of erzählen (‘to tell, recount’).

sentences.t: Antwortete er „versprecht ihr mir auch daß das erste Mädchen, das uns begegnet, nicht soll getödtet werden?“
sentences.l: He answered, “do you also promise me that the first girl who meets us shall not be killed?”
sentences.i: He answered, “Do you also promise me that the first girl we meet shall not be killed?”
sentences.n: “versprecht” = you promise (versprechen, 2nd pl.). “getödtet” keeps the old spelling (modern getötet).

sentences.t: „Ja,“ riefen sie alle, „das soll Gnade haben, erzähl uns nur.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” they all cried, “that shall have mercy, only tell us.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” they all cried, “she shall be spared — only tell us.”
sentences.n: “Gnade haben” = to be shown mercy, be spared. “erzähl” is the bare imperative (‘tell!’); “nur” softens/urges, ‘just, only.’

sentences.t: Da sprach er „unsere Schwester ist da,“ und hub die Bütte auf, und die Königstochter kam hervor in ihren königlichen Kleidern mit dem goldenen Stern auf der Stirne, und war so schön, zart und fein.
sentences.l: Then he spoke, “our sister is here,” and lifted up the tub, and the king’s daughter came forth in her royal clothes with the golden star on her forehead, and was so beautiful, delicate and fine.
sentences.i: Then he said, “Our sister is here,” and lifted up the tub, and the king’s daughter came forth in her royal robes with the golden star on her brow, and was so beautiful, delicate and fine.
sentences.n: “hub ... auf” is the archaic preterite of aufheben (‘to lift up’); modern German uses hob auf. “kam hervor” (hervorkommen) = came forth. “zart und fein” = delicate and fine.

sentences.t: Da freueten sie sich alle, fielen ihr um den Hals und küßten sie und hatten sie vom Herzen lieb.
sentences.l: Then they all rejoiced, fell about her neck and kissed her and held her dear from the heart.
sentences.i: Then they all rejoiced, fell upon her neck and kissed her, and loved her from the bottom of their hearts.
sentences.n: “freueten” is an older form of freuten (sich freuen, ‘to rejoice’). “jemandem um den Hals fallen” = to fall on someone’s neck, embrace. “jemanden vom Herzen lieb haben” = to love dearly, from the heart.

sentences.t: Nun blieb sie bei Benjamin zu Haus und half ihm in der Arbeit.
sentences.l: Now she stayed at home with Benjamin and helped him in the work.
sentences.i: Now she stayed at home with Benjamin and helped him with the work.

sentences.t: Die elfe zogen in den Wald, fiengen Gewild, Rehe, Vögel und Täuberchen, damit sie zu essen hatten, und die Schwester und Benjamin sorgten daß es zubereitet wurde.
sentences.l: The eleven went off into the wood, caught game, roe-deer, birds and little doves, so that they had (something) to eat, and the sister and Benjamin took care that it was prepared.
sentences.i: The eleven went off into the forest and caught game — roe, birds and young doves — so that they had food, and the sister and Benjamin saw to it that it was made ready.
sentences.n: “die elfe” = the eleven (of them). “fiengen” is the 1857 spelling of fingen (fangen, ‘to catch’). “Gewild” = game, wild animals (a collective). “sorgten daß” = took care / saw to it that; “zubereitet” = prepared.

sentences.t: Sie suchte das Holz zum Kochen und die Kräuter zum Gemüs, und stellte die Töpfe ans Feuer, also daß die Mahlzeit immer fertig war, wenn die Elfe kamen.
sentences.l: She sought the wood for cooking and the herbs for the vegetables, and set the pots on the fire, so that the meal was always ready when the eleven came.
sentences.i: She gathered the wood for cooking and the herbs for the greens, and set the pots on the fire, so that the meal was always ready when the eleven came.
sentences.n: “zum Kochen / zum Gemüs” = for the cooking / for the vegetables. “also daß” = so that. “die Elfe” = the eleven (brothers).

sentences.t: Sie hielt auch sonst Ordnung im Häuschen, und deckte die Bettlein hübsch weiß und rein, und die Brüder waren immer zufrieden und lebten in großer Einigkeit mit ihr.
sentences.l: She also otherwise kept order in the cottage, and covered the little beds prettily white and clean, and the brothers were always content and lived in great concord with her.
sentences.i: She kept the cottage in good order too, and made up the little beds prettily white and clean, and the brothers were always content and lived in great harmony with her.
sentences.n: “sonst” here = ‘otherwise, besides.’ “Bettlein” = little beds (diminutive). “Einigkeit” = unity, concord, harmony — a stock word for the family peace, and the tale ends on it.

sentences.t: Auf eine Zeit hatten die beiden daheim eine schöne Kost zurecht gemacht, und wie sie nun alle beisammen waren, setzten sie sich, aßen und tranken und waren voller Freude.
sentences.l: At one time the two at home had made ready a fine meal, and when they were now all together, they sat down, ate and drank and were full of joy.
sentences.i: One time the two of them had prepared a fine meal at home, and when they were all together, they sat down, ate and drank, and were full of joy.
sentences.n: “Auf eine Zeit” = at one time, once. “Kost” = fare, food, meal. “zurecht gemacht” = made ready, prepared. “beisammen” = together.

sentences.t: Es war aber ein kleines Gärtchen an dem verwünschten Häuschen, darin standen zwölf Lilienblumen, die man auch Studenten heißt: nun wollte sie ihren Brüdern ein Vergnügen machen, brach die zwölf Blumen ab und dachte jedem aufs Essen eine zu schenken.
sentences.l: But there was a little garden by the enchanted cottage, in which stood twelve lily-flowers, which one also calls ‘students’: now she wanted to give her brothers a pleasure, broke off the twelve flowers and thought to give each one for his meal.
sentences.i: But there was a little garden by the enchanted cottage, in which stood twelve lilies, which people also call ‘students’; now she wished to give her brothers a treat, and broke off the twelve flowers, meaning to lay one by each brother’s place at table.
sentences.n: “Gärtchen” = little garden (diminutive). “Lilienblumen ... Studenten” — a folk name for the flower; Grimm notes people called this white lily ‘Studenten.’ “brach ... ab” (abbrechen) = broke off, plucked, separable. “ein Vergnügen machen” = to give (someone) a pleasure/treat.

sentences.t: Wie sie aber die Blumen abgebrochen hatte, in demselben Augenblick waren die zwölf Brüder in zwölf Raben verwandelt und flogen über den Wald hin fort, und das Haus mit dem Garten war auch verschwunden.
sentences.l: But when she had broken off the flowers, in the same moment the twelve brothers were changed into twelve ravens and flew away over the wood, and the house with the garden had vanished too.
sentences.i: But the moment she had broken off the flowers, the twelve brothers were turned into twelve ravens and flew away over the forest, and the house with its garden had vanished as well.
sentences.n: “abgebrochen” is the past participle of abbrechen (separable ab-). “in Raben verwandelt” — the enchantment turns the brothers into ravens; “verwandeln in” = to transform into. “flogen ... hin fort” = flew away.

sentences.t: Da war nun das arme Mädchen allein in dem wilden Wald, und wie es sich umsah, so stand eine alte Frau neben ihm, die sprach „mein Kind, was hast du angefangen? warum hast du die zwölf weißen Blumen nicht stehen lassen? das waren deine Brüder, die sind nun auf immer in Raben verwandelt.“
sentences.l: Then the poor girl was now alone in the wild wood, and as it looked around, an old woman stood beside her, who spoke, “my child, what have you started? why did you not leave the twelve white flowers standing? those were your brothers, who are now forever changed into ravens.”
sentences.i: Now the poor girl was alone in the wild forest, and as she looked around, an old woman was standing beside her, who said, “My child, what have you done? Why did you not leave the twelve white flowers standing? Those were your brothers, and now they are turned into ravens forever.”
sentences.n: “wie es sich umsah” — sich umsehen (‘to look around’), separable, past umsah. “was hast du angefangen?” — anfangen literally ‘to begin,’ but idiomatically ‘what have you done / gotten yourself into.’ “stehen lassen” = to leave standing. “auf immer” = forever.

sentences.t: Das Mädchen sprach weinend „ist denn kein Mittel sie zu erlösen?“
sentences.l: The girl spoke, weeping, “is there then no means to redeem them?”
sentences.i: The girl said, weeping, “Is there then no way to deliver them?”
sentences.n: “weinend” = weeping (present participle of weinen). “denn” is a flavoring particle, ‘then, really.’ “Mittel” = means, remedy. “erlösen” recurs — to redeem, free from the spell.

sentences.t: „Nein,“ sagte die Alte, „es ist keins auf der ganzen Welt als eins, das ist aber so schwer, daß du sie damit nicht befreien wirst, denn du mußt sieben Jahre stumm sein, darfst nicht sprechen und nicht lachen, und sprichst du ein einziges Wort, und es fehlt nur eine Stunde an den sieben Jahren, so ist alles umsonst, und deine Brüder werden von dem einen Wort getödtet.“
sentences.l: “No,” said the old woman, “there is none in the whole world but one, but it is so hard that you will not free them by it, for you must be mute for seven years, may not speak and not laugh, and if you speak a single word, and but one hour is lacking of the seven years, then all is in vain, and your brothers are killed by that one word.”
sentences.i: “No,” said the old woman, “there is none in all the world but one, and that one is so hard that you will not free them by it: for you must be mute for seven years — you may not speak and not laugh — and if you speak a single word, even with only one hour wanting of the seven years, then all is in vain, and your brothers will be killed by that one word.”
sentences.n: This is the tale’s central vow: seven years mute, no speaking and no laughing, or the brothers die — the same self-silencing test drives KHM 49 (The Six Swans). “darfst nicht” = you may not (dürfen). “sprichst du ein ... Wort” — verb-first conditional, ‘if you speak.’ “es fehlt ... an” = there is lacking / wanting. “umsonst” = in vain.

sentences.t: Da sprach das Mädchen in seinem Herzen „ich weiß gewiß daß ich meine Brüder erlöse,“ und gieng und suchte einen hohen Baum, setzte sich darauf und spann, und sprach nicht und lachte nicht.
sentences.l: Then the girl spoke in her heart, “I know for certain that I will redeem my brothers,” and went and sought a high tree, seated herself upon it and spun, and spoke not and laughed not.
sentences.i: Then the girl said in her heart, “I know for certain that I shall deliver my brothers,” and went and looked for a tall tree, sat down in it and span, and did not speak and did not laugh.
sentences.n: “in seinem Herzen” — seinem is neuter agreeing with das Mädchen (‘in her heart’). “gewiß” = certainly, for sure. “erlöse” is present tense used for the future resolve. “spann” is the preterite of spinnen (‘to spin’). Note the vow now in force: sprach nicht und lachte nicht.

sentences.t: Nun trugs sich zu, daß ein König in dem Walde jagte, der hatte einen großen Windhund, der lief zu dem Baum, wo das Mädchen drauf saß, sprang herum, schrie und bellte hinauf.
sentences.l: Now it came about that a king was hunting in the wood; he had a great greyhound that ran to the tree where the girl sat upon, sprang about, cried and barked upward.
sentences.i: Now it came to pass that a king was hunting in the forest; he had a great greyhound that ran to the tree the girl was sitting in, leapt about, yelped and barked up at her.
sentences.n: “trugs sich zu” = it came about (sich zutragen; trugs = trug es). “Windhund” = greyhound. “wo das Mädchen drauf saß” = the tree the girl was sitting on (drauf = darauf).

sentences.t: Da kam der König herbei und sah die schöne Königstochter mit dem goldenen Stern auf der Stirne, und war so entzückt über ihre Schönheit, daß er ihr zurief ob sie seine Gemahlin werden wollte.
sentences.l: Then the king came up and saw the beautiful king’s daughter with the golden star on her forehead, and was so enraptured over her beauty that he called to her whether she would become his consort.
sentences.i: Then the king came up and saw the beautiful king’s daughter with the golden star on her brow, and was so enchanted by her beauty that he called up to her, asking whether she would become his wife.
sentences.n: “kam ... herbei” (herbeikommen) = came up, approached. “entzückt über” = delighted / enraptured by. “zurief” (zurufen, + dative) = called out to. “Gemahlin” = wife, consort.

sentences.t: Sie gab keine Antwort, nickte aber ein wenig mit dem Kopf.
sentences.l: She gave no answer, but nodded a little with her head.
sentences.i: She gave no answer, but nodded her head a little.
sentences.n: Bound by her vow of silence, she can only nod. “mit dem Kopf nicken” = to nod one’s head.

sentences.t: Da stieg er selbst auf den Baum, trug sie herab, setzte sie auf sein Pferd und führte sie heim.
sentences.l: Then he himself climbed up the tree, carried her down, set her on his horse and led her home.
sentences.i: Then he climbed up the tree himself, carried her down, set her on his horse, and took her home.
sentences.n: “trug ... herab” (herabtragen) = carried down. “führte ... heim” (heimführen) = led home — an old term for bringing a bride home.

sentences.t: Da ward die Hochzeit mit großer Pracht und Freude gefeiert: aber die Braut sprach nicht und lachte nicht.
sentences.l: Then the wedding was celebrated with great splendor and joy: but the bride spoke not and laughed not.
sentences.i: Then the wedding was celebrated with great splendour and joy — but the bride did not speak and did not laugh.
sentences.n: “ward ... gefeiert” = was celebrated (ward, old preterite of werden, forming the passive). The refrain “sprach nicht und lachte nicht” keeps the vow before us.

sentences.t: Als sie ein paar Jahre mit einander vergnügt gelebt hatten, fieng die Mutter des Königs, die eine böse Frau war, an, die junge Königin zu verläumden und sprach zum König „es ist ein gemeines Bettelmädchen, das du dir mitgebracht hast, wer weiß was für gottlose Streiche sie heimlich treibt.
sentences.l: When they had lived a couple of years contentedly with one another, the king’s mother, who was a wicked woman, began to slander the young queen and spoke to the king, “it is a common beggar-girl that you have brought yourself; who knows what godless tricks she secretly gets up to.
sentences.i: When they had lived contentedly together for a couple of years, the king’s mother, who was a wicked woman, began to slander the young queen, and said to the king, “She is a common beggar-girl you have brought home; who knows what godless tricks she is up to in secret.
sentences.n: “fieng ... an” (anfangen) = began, separable, split around the long clause. “verläumden” is the 1857 spelling of verleumden (‘to slander’). “gemeines” = common, base. “Bettelmädchen” = beggar-girl. “Streiche treiben” = to play tricks / get up to mischief (treibt).

sentences.t: Wenn sie stumm ist und nicht sprechen kann, so könnte sie doch einmal lachen, aber wer nicht lacht, der hat ein böses Gewissen.“
sentences.l: If she is mute and cannot speak, she could still laugh sometime; but whoever does not laugh has a bad conscience.”
sentences.i: Even if she is mute and cannot speak, she could still laugh now and then; but whoever never laughs has a guilty conscience.”
sentences.n: “stumm” = mute, dumb, silent. “doch einmal” = at least once, all the same. The mother twists the vow of silence into evidence of guilt — the engine of the false accusation.

sentences.t: Der König wollte zuerst nicht daran glauben, aber die Alte trieb es so lange und beschuldigte sie so viel böser Dinge, daß der König sich endlich überreden ließ und sie zum Tod verurtheilte.
sentences.l: The king would not at first believe it, but the old woman drove it so long and accused her of so many wicked things, that the king finally let himself be persuaded and condemned her to death.
sentences.i: At first the king would not believe it, but the old woman pressed it so long and accused her of so many wicked things that at last the king let himself be persuaded, and condemned her to death.
sentences.n: “daran glauben” = to believe in it. “trieb es so lange” = kept pressing / driving it so long. “beschuldigte ... böser Dinge” = accused her of wicked things (genitive of the charge). “sich überreden lassen” = to let oneself be talked round. “verurtheilte” has the old -th- (modern verurteilte).

sentences.t: Nun ward im Hof ein großes Feuer angezündet, darin sollte sie verbrannt werden: und der König stand oben am Fenster und sah mit weinenden Augen zu, weil er sie noch immer so lieb hatte.
sentences.l: Now a great fire was kindled in the courtyard, in which she was to be burned: and the king stood above at the window and looked on with weeping eyes, because he still loved her so.
sentences.i: Now a great fire was kindled in the courtyard, in which she was to be burned; and the king stood above at the window and watched with weeping eyes, because he still loved her so dearly.
sentences.n: “ward ... angezündet” = was kindled (passive with ward). “sah ... zu” (zusehen) = looked on, watched, separable. “mit weinenden Augen” = with weeping eyes. “lieb haben” = to love, hold dear.

sentences.t: Und als sie schon an den Pfahl festgebunden war, und das Feuer an ihren Kleidern mit rothen Zungen leckte, da war eben der letzte Augenblick von den sieben Jahren verflossen.
sentences.l: And when she was already bound fast to the stake, and the fire licked at her clothes with red tongues, then just the last moment of the seven years had elapsed.
sentences.i: And when she was already bound fast to the stake, and the fire was licking at her clothes with red tongues, at that very instant the last moment of the seven years had run out.
sentences.n: “an den Pfahl festgebunden” = bound fast to the stake (festbinden, separable). “mit rothen Zungen leckte” — the flames ‘lick with red tongues’ (rothen keeps old -th-; Zungen = tongues). “verflossen” = elapsed, run out (verfließen, of time). The deadline lands at the last possible instant.

sentences.t: Da ließ sich in der Luft ein Geschwirr hören, und zwölf Raben kamen hergezogen und senkten sich nieder: und wie sie die Erde berührten, waren es ihre zwölf Brüder, die sie erlöst hatte.
sentences.l: Then a whirring made itself heard in the air, and twelve ravens came flying up and sank themselves down: and when they touched the earth, it was her twelve brothers, whom she had redeemed.
sentences.i: Then a whirring was heard in the air, and twelve ravens came flying up and sank down; and as they touched the earth, they were her twelve brothers, whom she had delivered.
sentences.n: “ließ sich ... hören” = made itself heard, was heard. “Geschwirr” = a whirring, whirr of wings. “kamen hergezogen” (herziehen) = came flying / drawing near. “senkten sich nieder” (sich niedersenken) = sank down. “berührten” = touched. The vow is fulfilled at the last instant, and the enchantment breaks.

sentences.t: Sie rissen das Feuer auseinander, löschten die Flammen, machten ihre liebe Schwester frei, und küßten und herzten sie.
sentences.l: They tore the fire apart, put out the flames, made their dear sister free, and kissed and embraced her.
sentences.i: They pulled the fire apart, quenched the flames, set their dear sister free, and kissed and hugged her.
sentences.n: “rissen ... auseinander” (auseinanderreißen) = tore apart. “löschten die Flammen” = put out / quenched the flames. “frei machen” = to set free.

sentences.t: Nun aber, da sie ihren Mund aufthun und reden durfte, erzählte sie dem Könige warum sie stumm gewesen wäre und niemals gelacht hätte.
sentences.l: But now, since she was allowed to open her mouth and speak, she told the king why she had been mute and had never laughed.
sentences.i: But now, since she was free to open her mouth and speak, she told the king why she had been mute and had never laughed.
sentences.n: “aufthun” is the 1857 spelling of auftun (‘to open,’ of the mouth), separable. “dem Könige” has the old dative -e. “gewesen wäre ... gelacht hätte” — subjunctive of reported speech (she explains why she *had been* mute), everywhere in Grimm.

sentences.t: Der König freute sich als er hörte daß sie unschuldig war, und sie lebten nun alle zusammen in Einigkeit bis an ihren Tod.
sentences.l: The king rejoiced when he heard that she was innocent, and they now all lived together in concord until their death.
sentences.i: The king rejoiced when he heard that she was innocent, and they all now lived together in harmony until their death.
sentences.n: “unschuldig” = innocent, guiltless. “in Einigkeit ... bis an ihren Tod” = in concord until their death — the tale closes on Einigkeit, the family harmony it began with.

sentences.t: Die böse Stiefmutter ward vor Gericht gestellt, und in ein Faß gesteckt, das mit siedendem Oel und giftigen Schlangen angefüllt war, und starb eines bösen Todes.
sentences.l: The wicked stepmother was brought before the court, and put into a barrel that was filled with boiling oil and poisonous snakes, and died an evil death.
sentences.i: The wicked stepmother was brought before the court and put into a barrel filled with boiling oil and venomous snakes, and died an evil death.
sentences.n: The king’s mother is now called “Stiefmutter” (stepmother). “vor Gericht stellen” = to bring before the court. “siedendem Oel” = boiling oil (Oel is the 1857 spelling of Öl). “angefüllt” = filled up. “starb eines bösen Todes” = died an evil death (genitive of manner) — the unbowdlerized Grimm punishment.
